Judgment of Full Court V.L.R., 51; 26 A.L.T, 103, affirmed, In re Wiedeman, 5 V-L.R. (LLP. & M.), 32, considered, Appear from the Supreme Court of Vietoria, On the 5th September, 1904, Beath, Schiess & Co. obtained a order nisi for the sequestration of the estate of George Dobson, the act of insolvency alleged being " That the said Frank Dobson did, on the 7th day of July, 1904, make a conve. ance or assignment of his property for the benefit of his creditors generally." 'The conveyance or assignment in question was an indenture expressed to be made between George Frank Dobson, the debtor, of the first part, Edward Graham, the trustee, of second part, and "the several persons, companies and partners firms being creditors of the debtor whose names and seals are and affixed in the schedule hereto or who shall otherwise assent to these presents and who are hereinafter called the creditors of the third part." By the indenture the debtor purported to grant, release, convey, and assign and transfer unto the trustee all the Property of the debtor.
citation: Dobson v Beath Schiess and Co [1905] HCA 4

text: It was declared that the trustee should stand possessed of all moneys to arise upon sale and conversion of the property upon trust, after payment of charges and expenses, "to apply the residue of the same moneys in or towards payment of the debts and sums of money owing by the debtor to the Persons and parties whose names appear as his creditors respeee tively in the schedule hereto and to all others (if any) of the creditors of the debtor who shall by reasonable efforts in that behalf satisfy the trustees or trustee that he or they was or were entitled at the date of these presents to be included as a ereditor or ereditors respectively without any priority or preference what Soever, and in due course of administration." 'There was alsoa Proviso in these words: " Provided always that the trustees ot trustee shall not be precluded by anything in the schedule hereto contained from inquiring into and insisting upon such proof as he or they shall deem reasonable in support of any debt alleged in such schedule to be due to any person or persons therein named as a creditor or creditors, and that the said trustees or trustee OF AUSTRALIA. mt be bound or required to pay any dividend on any vent inserted in the said schedule in excess of the amount shal hy reasonable evidence in that behalf be shown to have nue or owing at the day of the date hereof." 'The indenture eration of the premises the said several persons and corconsid ions parties hereto of the third part do and each of them mom all and all manner of actions suits claims and demands ver either at law or in equity which they respectively have or at any time may hereafter have against the said iiior his executors or administrators for or by reason or on ant of the several debts or sums of money due owing or aing due and owing to them respectively by the said debtor foresaid. Provided always that the release hereinbefore coned shall be inoperative and have no validity either at law or ity if these presents be not registered in accordance with orif these presents be at any time set aside." the return of the order nisi an objection was taken (inter that the deed was not for the benefit of creditors generally, ing regard to the terms of the direction given to the trustee toyayment of creditors who should satisfy the trustee that 'Bult J. stated a case for the opinion of the Full Court, by ihe asked whether, having regard to the objection above e Full Court (u'Beckett and Hood JJ., Madden CJ. dising) having answered this question in the affirmative (1), Mlett J. made the order absolute. this decision the debtor appealed to the Full Court, and having been dismissed, he now appealed from the mof the Full Court to the High Court. In re Wiedeman (1) had been preceded by three decisions of the same judge, viz., Port v. London Chartered Bank (3), in which it yas held that a deed for the benefit of all creditors who should sign it within a certain time, was not for the benefit of creditors generally, In re Derham (4), and In re Haslam (5), in both of which it was held that a deed for the benefit of scheduled creditors was not for the benefit of creditors generally.

text: In re Wiedeman (1) was approved and followed in In re Thomas and Cowie (6) and in Beeston v. Donaldson (7). In this state of the law the Insolvency Act 1897 was passed, and no alteration was made as to this particular matter.

text: So that ereditors who are named in the schedule are liable to be called upon to prove their debts just as fully as creditors not named in the schedule, but becoming parties to the deed hy assenting to it. The only other part of the deed to which it is necessary to refer is the final clause, which contains the release by the parties of the third part (who include all creditors who assent to th deed), and continues: " Provided always that the release herein before contained shall be inoperative and have no validity either at law or in equity if these presents be not registered in accordant with law or if these presents be at any time set aside." Tt obvious from the concluding clause that it was intended that | the deed should be registered under the Insolvency Act 180. | 'The deed therefore was on the face of it intended to be a deed to which all creditors who think fit to assent—who may "assent to or take the benefit of " it, in the words of the Statute—would be parties, :: As Ihave said, the contention is that the trustee has never absolute power to exclude any creditor from the benefit of the deed. If that be the true construction, no doubt the deed is not for the benefit of creditors generally.

text: HIGH COURT the event of any such creditor or creditors refusing or fail to verify his or their debt or debts, or declining to execute presents, then such creditor or creditors so refusing or fa declining as aforesaid, shall lose all benefit, dividends and adva tage to be derived from or otherwise claimed under these re sents." On that clause being relied on to invalidate the ded "the Lord Chancellor pointed out that this clause was non it enabled the trustee to require the amount of debt of any of creditors parties thereto to be verified, and in the event of md it is open to two constructions, one that the trustee may at option, acting as an arbitrator, reject those of the ereditors who do not satisfy him that they are creditors; the other that h exercises that power subject to the control of the proper Ie tribunal, which in the absence of legislation would be a Courtof Equity. Having regard to those principles and the obviousintention of the parties to this deed, it seems to me that the proper construction is that the deed does not enable the trustee to exelule any of the creditors. Therefore it is an assignment for th benetit of creditors generally, and is within the definition of th Statute.

text: Under see. 83 of the Znsolvency Act 1897, the trustee of a deed ovomers. this kind is an officer of the Court, so that everything he dy in the administration of his trust must be done under the oui ance of the Court, and he is responsible to the Court for even thing he does. Under sec. 79 the provisions of the Ins Acts as to the payment of certain claims as preferential, as the proof of debts, as to the respective rights of secured an unsecured creditors, and as to the examination of the debtor o any other person are to apply to every deed of arrangement, that this trustee is not at all in the position in which at was before the passing of that Act. By the fact of his beeo a trustee under the deed, he becomes an officer of the Court, the estate is to be distributed in accordance with the pro of the Insolvency Acts.

text: We find, for instance, that section regulating the proof of debts, and as to the appeal from that proof, are amongst those to be applied in the adiinistration of th estate by the trustee. In other words the whole of Patt ¥ Division 3 of the Insolvency Act 1890 as to proof of debts becomes incorporated as part of the duties and obligations to be performel by the trustee under the deed of assignment, Under see. 106d that Aet, proof is to be by affidavit containing a complete statem of account between the creditor and the insolvent, and if that proof is rejected, there is an appeal under see. 109 to the Court, and "t Court may at any time admit, reject, expunge or reduce a prot of debt on the application of any ereditor or of the trustee orofthe insolvent." Now it appears to me that we must read this deed) the light of those provisions, because it is apparent that, as thede is to be worked under the Insolvency Acts—as it has to be tered under them—it is intended by it to put the trustee in sucha position that he will be endowed with the powers and bunden#l with the obligations which a trustee has under those Acts | consideration of these matters throws a great deal of light up the portion of the deed on which most of the argument hit turned, that is to say, the diseretion which the trustee has in OF AUSTRALIA. i. fat all, that discretion shall be controlled. In other ds it is apparent on the face of the deed that it was Jr ajed to clothe the trustee, not with an uncontrolled power to ithe Court under the provisions of the Insolvency Acts. " Before I refer to the words of the deed giving these powers, I hhink it may be useful to consider what would be the position of the trustee if the words of the deed I have referred to were left at altogether.

text: That being so, I think the ded is one which on the face of it, and in accordance with its ante tion, is an assignment for the benefit of all the ereditors of th insolvent. T could hardly imagine that any doubt could be raised on th matter, if it had not been for the decision of In re Wiedeman I wish to add nothing to what the learned Chief Justice has in reference to that case, except this, that in considering Jn Wiedeman we must have regard to the condition of the la the time it was decided. In 1879 a deed of assignment was nd administered under the Court as it is under the Act of 18 'There was no doubt a remedy against the trustee, as against other trustee, in a Court of Equity, But the trustee was not at officer of the Court,and there were not the same remedies him as under the Act of 1897.
citation: Dobson v Beath Schiess and Co [1905] HCA 4

text: I think that alone is sufficient to enable us to say that the decision in Jn re Wiedeman, having regard to the provisions of the deed as set out in the report, and having regard to the law at that time, was right But this des, which must be read in accordance with the law as it exists nov, puts the trustee in quite a different position, and therefore th considerations in In re Wiedeman cannot be applied to this For these reasons I think the decision of the Full Court wastight Appeal dismissed with costs. Solicitor for appellant, W. Brocket, Melbourne.
citation: Dobson v Beath Schiess and Co [1905] HCA 4
